title: Cat Squinting Left Eye: Causes, Triage, and Care Tips
meta_desc: Is your cat squinting their left eye? Learn about common causes, urgency levels, and how to help your vet with triage photos.
content_html:
TL;DR: Squinting in one eye often signals minor irritation or pain; while usually low urgency, you should monitor your cat and seek veterinary care if symptoms persist beyond 24 hours or the eye appears cloudy.

What does it mean if my cat is squinting in one eye?

  • Squinting is a clinical sign known as blepharospasm, typically caused by pain, discomfort, or light sensitivity.
  • It often indicates a response to minor irritations like dust particles, small corneal scratches, or mild allergic reactions.
  • This reaction is the body's natural way of protecting the globe of the eye from further irritation.

Is my cat squinting one eye a veterinary emergency?

  • The urgency level is generally low if your cat is still eating, playing, and lacks significant swelling or thick discharge.
  • You can typically monitor the eye for a few hours if no other symptoms are present.
  • A veterinary exam is necessary if squinting persists for more than 24 hours or if the eye appears cloudy, which may indicate infections or ulcers.

How does a photo help my vet triage my cat's eye issue?

  • A high-resolution photo speeds up the assessment by allowing professionals to look for redness, discharge, or foreign objects.
  • It helps the triage process without causing the cat additional stress from a physical exam.
  • It serves as a baseline to track whether the condition is improving or worsening over time.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats presenting with a squinting left eye, potential causes include conjunctivitis (unilateral or beginning unilaterally before becoming bilateral), often due to feline herpesvirus-1 (FHV-1), Mycoplasma, or Chlamydia psittaci. Other considerations include foreign bodies, dacryocystitis, keratoconjunctivitis sicca, eyelid inflammation associated with systemic Demodex cati, D gatoi, Notoedres cati (scabies), ringworm, or immune-mediated skin diseases. Specific diagnosis of conjunctivitis etiologies is made via history, physical examination, conjunctival scrapings and culture, Schirmer tear test, and occasionally biopsy, demonstrating inclusions or the agent in conjunctival scrapings. Neoplasia, while less frequent in cats than dogs, should also be considered, particularly squamous cell carcinoma in white cats with nonpigmented eyelid margins. Systemic diseases such as feline infectious peritonitis, toxoplasmosis, feline immunodeficiency virus, and feline leukemia virus, which can present as anterior and posterior uveitis should also be considered.

Chapter: Ophthalmology, Infectious Disease

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 492)

faqs.question: Can I use human eye drops on my cat?
faqs.answer: No, you should never use human medications or over-the-counter eye drops on a cat unless specifically directed by a veterinarian, as some ingredients can be toxic or worsen certain conditions.

faqs.question: What are common signs of eye irritation in cats?
faqs.answer: Common signs include squinting, excessive tearing, redness of the white part of the eye, pawing at the face, and a visible third eyelid.

faqs.question: How should I clean my cat's eye?
faqs.answer: If there is mild discharge, you can gently wipe the area around the eye with a soft cloth dampened with warm water. Do not touch the eyeball itself.
